name: Yang, Shiyan
recent_1: Yang, S., Wilson, K. M., Shiferaw, B., Roady, T., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2024). Sensor fusion to connect gaze fixation with dynamic driving context for driver attention management. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 107, 578–588. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2024.07.025
recent_2: Mulhall, M., Wilson, K., Yang, S., Kuo, J., Sletten, T., Anderson, C., Howard, M. E., Rajaratnam, S., Magee, M., Collins, A., & Lenné, M. G. (2023). European NCAP driver state monitoring protocols: Prevalence of distraction in naturalistic driving. Human Factors, 66(8), 2024. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231194543
recent_3: Yang, S., McKerral, A., Mulhall, M. D., Lenné, M. G., Reimer, B., & Gershon, P. (2023). Takeover context matters: Characterising context of takeovers in naturalistic driving using Super Cruise and Autopilot. Proceedings of the 15th International Conference on Automotive User Interfaces and Interactive Vehicular Applications (AutomotiveUI '23), 112–122. DOI: 10.1145/3580585.3606459
recent_1_synopsis: This study fuses interior driver-facing gaze signals with exterior driving-context sensing to interpret where and why drivers look, moving beyond isolated gaze fixation toward context-aware driver attention management. It demonstrates how sensor fusion can connect a fixation to the dynamic scene element that warrants attention, informing more meaningful real-time distraction and attention-allocation assessment.
recent_2_synopsis: Applying European NCAP driver state monitoring protocols to a large naturalistic driving dataset, this work quantifies how often distraction (and its prescribed visual/cognitive categories) actually occurs on the road. It provides empirical prevalence estimates that ground regulatory DSM testing protocols in real-world driver behavior.
recent_3_synopsis: Using a naturalistic multimodal dataset of GM Super Cruise and Tesla Autopilot driving, this paper characterizes the situational context surrounding control takeovers in Level 2 systems. It shows that takeover behavior cannot be understood in isolation from driving context, with implications for designing takeover requests and evaluating driver readiness in partially automated vehicles.
seminal_1: Wilson, K. M., Yang, S., Roady, T., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2020). Driver trust & mode confusion in an on-road study of level-2 automated vehicle technology. Safety Science, 130, 104845. DOI: 10.1016/j.ssci.2020.104845
seminal_2: Yang, S., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2021). Effects of distraction in on-road level 2 automated driving: Impacts on glance behavior and takeover performance. Human Factors, 63(8), 1485–1497. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820936793
seminal_1_synopsis: Drawing on an on-road study of consumer Level 2 automation, this paper documents how drivers' trust calibrates over real driving and how mode confusion arises when drivers misunderstand which automation functions are active. With roughly 114 citations (his most-cited work), it is widely referenced for empirical, naturalistic evidence on trust and mode awareness in partially automated vehicles.
seminal_2_synopsis: This on-road experiment measured how secondary-task distraction during Level 2 automated driving degrades glance behavior and subsequent takeover performance. With approximately 60 citations, it is a foundational empirical demonstration that distraction in supervised automation impairs the very monitoring drivers are expected to perform, frequently cited in driver-distraction and takeover-readiness research.
expertise_overview: Shiyan Yang studies driver state monitoring at the intersection of human factors and machine learning, with a focus on detecting driver distraction, cognitive workload, drowsiness, and readiness to take over control. His work spans manual, Level 2, and conditionally automated driving, examining glance and gaze behavior, peripheral vision, takeover performance, and trust and mode confusion in partially automated vehicles. Methodologically he combines naturalistic and on-road driving studies with driving-simulator experiments and multimodal physiological sensing—including ECG-based workload detection and eye-tracking—and applies probabilistic, deep-learning, and generative modeling to noisy real-world driver data. A recurring theme is sensor fusion that connects driver-facing signals with the external driving context to make attention assessment context-aware. His applied domains include automotive-grade driver monitoring systems, regulatory protocols such as European NCAP DSM, and connected/automated truck platooning.
notes: Identification confidence: high. Disambiguated from the unrelated 'Jing Yang' (physiological sensing of situation awareness, ICU/surgical) — this Shiyan Yang is a driving-research human factors scientist. Identity confirmed via his personal site (shiyanyang.github.io), Google Scholar (user=JshgP2bDXhYC), and ResearchGate (profile/Shiyan-Yang); PhD Texas A&M 2016 (advisor Thomas Ferris), postdoc UC Berkeley PATH, Research/Senior Research Scientist at Seeing Machines (2017–2024), now Senior Data Scientist at Mutinex. The four anchor Human Factors papers match his profile exactly. Citation counts from Google Scholar as of June 2026 (total ~656, h-index 15): seminal_1 'Driver trust & mode confusion' ~114 (his most-cited), seminal_2 'Effects of distraction' ~60. All DOIs verified via Crossref: recent_1 (10.1016/j.trf.2024.07.025), recent_2 (10.1177/00187208231194543, pre-verified), recent_3 (10.1145/3580585.3606459), seminal_1 (10.1016/j.ssci.2020.104845), seminal_2 (10.1177/0018720820936793, pre-verified). recent_2's Human Factors print volume/issue (66(8), 2024) is approximate; online-first published 2023-08-20. No unverifiable citations.
